message.content:
You are an adversarial skeptic. Try to REFUTE this pass-8 finding about /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay by reading the actual code. A finding is REFUTED if it is a false positive, already-mitigated, out-of-scope by design (dataset-agnostic engine, optional import-guarded backends, intentionally-committed scrubbed transcripts, data-sovereign on-box), or not a real production defect. Default to REFUTED if the claim does not hold up. Use "python -m pytest" / "source .venv/bin/activate" if you test.

FINDING K-OBS-1 (high, dim OBS): SIGTERM trace-flush handler makes the study process unkillable by SIGTERM (swallows default terminate)
Location: src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:148-156 (_install_flush_on_exit._handler)
Claim: After bootstrap_tracing() runs on the main thread, it installs a SIGTERM handler that flushes/shuts down the tracer and then only re-invokes the prior handler `if callable(prior)`. Python's default SIGTERM disposition is signal.SIG_DFL == 0, which is NOT callable, so the chain falls through and the process does NOT terminate on SIGTERM. A `kill <pid>` / `docker stop` / k8s pod SIGTERM flushes traces but leaves the process running, requiring SIGKILL.
Why real: Reproduced directly: with prior=signal.getsignal(SIGTERM)=0 (SIG_DFL, callable()==False), sending os.kill(pid, SIGTERM) flushed but the process printed 'STILL ALIVE AFTER SIGTERM' and exited 0 only because the script ended. In a long-running study this converts graceful SIGTERM into a no-op; orchestrators that send SIGTERM-then-wait hang until their SIGKILL grace timeout. The fix-comment claims the handler 'also flushes+shuts down' but never preserves the default-terminate semantics, silently breaking normal process termination for every traced run.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

136	def _install_flush_on_exit(provider: Any) -> None:
137	    """Flush buffered spans on clean exit (atexit) and on SIGTERM (#O3).
138	
139	    atexit does not run on ``kill``/container-stop, so a main-thread SIGTERM handler also
140	    flushes+shuts down. Both are guarded — tracing teardown must never raise.
141	    """
142	    flush = getattr(provider, "force_flush", None)
143	    if not callable(flush):
144	        return
145	    atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush))
146	    if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread():
147	        try:
148	            prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)
149	
150	            def _handler(signum: int, frame: Any) -> None:
151	                _safe(flush)
152	                _safe(getattr(provider, "shutdown", lambda: None))
153	                if callable(prior):
154	                    prior(signum, frame)
155	
156	            signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)
157	        except (ValueError, OSError):  # pragma: no cover - not on main thread / unsupported
158	            pass

163	        fn()
164	    except Exception:

content.text: The finding is accurate about the code. Let me verify the claim about `signal.SIG_DFL` not being callable and the default disposition.

cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && python3 -c "
import signal
print('default SIGTERM disposition:', signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM))
print('SIG_DFL:', signal.SIG_DFL, 'callable:', callable(signal.SIG_DFL))
print('is default == SIG_DFL:', signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM) == signal.SIG_DFL)

content.text: The default SIGTERM disposition is `SIG_DFL`, which is NOT callable. So when the handler runs `if callable(prior): prior(...)`, the branch is skipped and the process does not terminate. Let me reproduce the actual behavior end to end.

content.content:

SHUTDOWN
STILL ALIVE AFTER SIGTERM
exit=0

content.text:
Confirmed. The handler flushes, shuts down, then does NOT terminate because `prior` (SIG_DFL) is not callable. The process prints "STILL ALIVE AFTER SIGTERM" — proof that SIGTERM no longer terminates.

Now let me check whether `bootstrap_tracing` is actually wired into the production study run path on the main thread, to confirm this isn't out-of-scope.

"""Self-hosted tracing — OpenTelemetry → a local collector (ADR-0003).

Ported and generalized from the prior platform's Phoenix/OTel bootstrap. Everything is
on-box: spans export over OTLP to a loopback-bound collector; nothing leaves the machine.
OpenTelemetry and the Phoenix collector are optional (the ``observability`` extra) and
imported lazily, so this module loads — and the engine runs untraced — with neither present.

The reasoning seam already emits spans through the global OTel tracer provider; calling
:func:`bootstrap_tracing` once at startup wires that global provider at a local collector so
those spans land somewhere. Without the extra (or with ``ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING`` set),
tracing degrades to a silent no-op.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import atexit
import logging
import os
import signal
import threading
from contextlib import contextmanager
from typing import Any, Iterator, Mapping, Protocol

from assay_engine._local import require_loopback_host

_log = logging.getLogger("assay_engine.observability")

# Local collector endpoint (loopback-enforced).
TRACING_HOST = os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRACING_HOST", "localhost")


def _int_env(name: str, default: str) -> int:
    """Parse an int env var with a clear error naming the var (#CV-S-1, same class as #H-022).

    A bare ``int(os.environ[...])`` at import time raises an opaque "invalid literal for int()"
    that names neither the var nor fires inside any handler.
    """
    raw = os.environ.get(name, default)
    try:
        return int(raw)
    except (ValueError, TypeError) as exc:
        raise ValueError(f"{name} must be an integer; got {raw!r}") from exc


TRACING_PORT = _int_env("ASSAY_TRACING_PORT", "6006")
PROJECT_NAME = os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRACING_PROJECT", "assay")

_provider: Any = None
# Guards the _provider check-then-set so concurrent bootstrap_tracing() calls don't race.
_provider_lock = threading.Lock()


def _disabled() -> bool:
    return os.environ.get("ASSAY_DISABLE_TRACING", "").strip().lower() in {"1", "true", "yes", "on"}


def tracing_base_url() -> str:
    require_loopback_host(TRACING_HOST, what="tracing collector host")
    return f"http://{TRACING_HOST}:{TRACING_PORT}"

content.text: bootstrap_tracing is called from the production study run path (pipeline.py:381, graph.py:111/139). Let me confirm it runs on the main thread there and isn't already guarded by the disabled/no-extra no-op for normal runs.

content.content:
67	def bootstrap_tracing() -> Any:
68	    """Idempotently wire the global OTel tracer provider to the local collector.
69	
70	    Returns the provider, or ``None`` if tracing is disabled, the extra is not installed, or
71	    setup fails — tracing must never be load-bearing, so this degrades gracefully.
72	    """
73	    global _provider
74	    if _disabled():
75	        return None
76	    # Lock the check-then-set so two threads can't both register the global provider.
77	    with _provider_lock:
78	        if _provider is not None:
79	            return _provider
80	        try:
81	            require_loopback_host(TRACING_HOST, what="tracing collector host")
82	            # Bound the OTLP export so a downed collector can never hang teardown (#O3).
83	            os.environ.setdefault("OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_TIMEOUT", "2")
84	            from phoenix.otel import register
85	
86	            provider = register(
87	                project_name=PROJECT_NAME,
88	                endpoint=tracing_endpoint(),
89	                protocol="http/protobuf",  # pin: endpoint is .../v1/traces — never flip to gRPC
90	                set_global_tracer_provider=True,
91	                batch=True,  # async BatchSpanProcessor — never block the run on export (#O2)
92	                verbose=False,  # no stdout banner from a library context
93	            )
94	            _provider = provider
95	            _warn_if_not_global(provider)
96	            _instrument_langchain()
97	            _install_flush_on_exit(provider)
98	            return _provider
99	        except Exception as exc:
100	            # Missing extra / collector down / registration race — run untraced. Graceful
101	            # degradation, but no longer SILENT (pass 3, #F-044): a misconfigured
102	            # ASSAY_TRACING_HOST/PORT or a missing observability extra now leaves a diagnosable
103	            # warning instead of an inexplicably untraced run.
104	            _log.warning(
105	                "assay tracing: setup failed, running untraced (%s: %s)",
106	                type(exc).__name__,
107	                exc,
108	            )
109	            return None

content.text:
The handler installs only when the observability extra is installed and tracing is enabled (the common production-traced path), and only on the main thread — which is exactly where pipeline.py:381 runs at study startup. The behavior is confirmed.

This is a real production defect: when the `observability` extra is present (the intended traced production configuration), a long-running study process installs a SIGTERM handler that flushes traces but never re-raises default-terminate semantics, so SIGTERM becomes a no-op. Orchestrators (docker stop, k8s) that send SIGTERM-then-wait will hang until the SIGKILL grace timeout. The out-of-scope exemptions (dataset-agnostic, optional import-guarded backends, scrubbed transcripts, on-box) do not apply — this is core process lifecycle behavior, not an optional backend concern, and even though tracing is optional, when present it breaks termination.

The finding stands.

input.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code at src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:136-158. The _install_flush_on_exit._handler runs _safe(flush) then _safe(shutdown), then `if callable(prior): prior(signum, frame)`. The default SIGTERM disposition is signal.SIG_DFL, which I confirmed at runtime is NOT callable (callable(signal.SIG_DFL) == False, and signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM) == SIG_DFL on a fresh process). Therefore the `callable(prior)` branch is skipped and the process never performs the default terminate action. I reproduced this end-to-end: a script replicating the exact handler logic, after os.kill(pid, SIGTERM), printed FLUSHED, SHUTDOWN, then STILL ALIVE AFTER SIGTERM and continued running — proving SIGTERM is swallowed into a no-op.

This is wired into the real production run path: bootstrap_tracing() is called at pipeline.py:381 (and orchestration/graph.py:111/139), invokes _install_flush_on_exit(provider) at tracing.py:97, which installs the handler when threading.current_thread() is the main thread — the study startup thread. The handler is only installed when tracing is enabled AND the observability extra/Phoenix register() succeeds (the intended traced production configuration). When present, a `kill <pid>`/`docker stop`/k8s pod SIGTERM flushes traces but leaves the long-running study process alive, forcing orchestrators to wait out their grace period and SIGKILL.

None of the REFUTED exemptions apply: this is not an optional-backend concern in spirit (it is core process-lifecycle behavior that fires precisely in the supported traced config), not dataset-specific, not test/transcript scope, and not a false positive. The docstring even claims the handler "flushes+shuts down" on SIGTERM but it silently fails to preserve default-terminate semantics. To fix, the default branch should re-raise default termination (e.g., reset to SIG_DFL and re-raise, or sys.exit/os._exit) when prior is SIG_DFL, and ignore when prior is SIG_IGN. Finding stands.

content.text: The finding K-OBS-1 STANDS. I confirmed via runtime reproduction that the SIGTERM handler at `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:150-154` swallows process termination because the default disposition `signal.SIG_DFL` is not callable, so the `if callable(prior)` guard skips the terminate. It is wired into the production run path via `bootstrap_tracing()` at `src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:381` on the main thread in the intended traced configuration.
